bibliographic_data.title: Reading Comprehension Ability Test-A Turing Test for Reading Comprehension
bibliographic_data.abstract: Reading comprehension is an important ability of human intelligence. In most education systems, developing reading comprehension ability is compulsory from year one to year 12. With the emergence of artificial intelligence, identifying the levels of machine comprehension ability becomes crucial for further improvement. This paper proposes a test called (reading) Comprehension Ability Test (CAT), which is similar to Turing test but includes multiple levels showing different abilities in reading comprehension, from identifying basic facts to understanding intent and sentiment.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper proposes a new test called Reading Comprehension Ability Test (CAT) which evaluates the ability of AI algorithms to comprehend text at different levels similar to human beings, addressing the limitations of previous Turing tests.
core_content.key_methodology: The CAT involves multiple levels of reading comprehension tasks, from identifying basic facts to understanding intent and sentiment. Each test consists of an article and a corresponding question set where both human and computer respondents need to answer.
outcomes: Developed a new test called Reading Comprehension Ability Test (CAT) to evaluate AI algorithms at different levels of reading comprehension.
Identified the limitations of current AI models in handling complex reading tasks.
core_content.full_text_markdown: Reading comprehension is an essential human intelligence that involves various abilities such as understanding word meanings, identifying main thoughts, and inferring writer's intent. In education systems, it is a critical skill for students to develop. The paper proposes the Reading Comprehension Ability Test (CAT) which evaluates AI algorithms at different levels of reading comprehension, similar to Turing test but more comprehensive. CAT includes multiple tests with articles and corresponding question sets where both human and computer respondents need to answer.
